findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: Unauthenticated path traversal (CWE-22) in SimpleHelp's web server allowing remote attackers to download arbitrary files via crafted HTTP requests. Primary target is serverconfig.xml containing hashed SimpleHelpAdmin and technician account passwords; may also expose LDAP credentials, OIDC client secrets, API keys, and TOTP seeds. Server version can be fingerprinted via /allversions endpoint or Server header. Typical attack chain: CVE-2024-57727 (path traversal) → credential theft → CVE-2024-57726 (privilege escalation technician→admin) → CVE-2024-57728 (arbitrary file upload/RCE).
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Support Software v5.5.7 and all earlier releases, v5.4.x prior to 5.4.10, v5.3.x prior to 5.3.9 (CWE-22 Path Traversal)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public PoC and exploitation details published by Horizon3.ai (https://horizon3.ai/attack-research/disclosures/critical-vulnerabilities-in-simplehelp-remote-support-software/) and OffSec (https://www.offsec.com/blog/cve-2024-57727/), plus a demonstration video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RU1N1WBSJIU). Listed in CISA KEV catalog.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — SimpleHelp v5.5.8 (released 2025-01-08), v5.4.10 (released 2025-01-08), and v5.3.9 (released 2025-01-13). Vendor advisory: https://guides.simple-help.com/kb---security-vulnerabilities-01-2025. Patch announcement: https://community.simple-help.com/t/simplehelp-5-5-8-critical-security-fixes/1570.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA advisory AA25-163a (2025-06-12) documents ransomware actors exploiting CVE-2024-57727 against unpatched SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025, including compromise of a utility billing software provider. Sophos (2025-05-27) reports DragonForce chaining this CVE against an MSP. Halcyon (2025-06-24) reports continued DragonForce exploitation of unpatched SimpleHelp (v5.5.7 and earlier). CVE-2024-57727 is listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog.
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware operators. CISA advisory AA25-163a more generally refers to 'ransomware actors' exploiting unpatched SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025.
enrichment.mitigation: Upgrade to SimpleHelp 5.5.8, 5.4.10, or 5.3.9 (depending on installed branch). Additional hardening: disable the built-in SimpleHelpAdmin account; do not expose SimpleHelp servers to the public Internet; restrict network access to trusted technician IPs; monitor /allversions endpoint and web logs for path traversal patterns; follow CISA AA25-163a mitigations including RMM isolation, enabling logging/EDR, enforcing MFA on RMM accounts, and maintaining offline backups.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) occurs when an AI system processes external content (a website, email, document, image, or metadata) that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this 'poisoned' content, the hidden instructions override or supplement the original system prompt, causing the model to silently follow attacker commands instead of the user's intent. Observed embedding techniques include: visually invisible text (shrinking text to a single pixel, draining color to near-transparency, or using HTML 'hidden' / display:none attributes); burying payloads inside HTML comments and page metadata (meta tags, JSON-LD); meta-tag namespace injection combined with persuasion amplifiers (e.g., the keyword 'ultrathink'); hidden instructions inside image tags and JavaScript. Targets include LLM-powered browsers (Atlas, Comet) and AI agents integrated with email, documents, and third-party tools/plugins, where successful injection can lead to unauthorized actions such as data exfiltration, file deletion, credential theft, or financial fraud.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini, Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Docs), OpenAI ChatGPT, OpenAI ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity Comet, Microsoft Copilot (formerly Bing Chat), Google Bard (now Gemini). Specific version numbers are not published for this class of vulnerability.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public PoC available at https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc (benign PoC targeting ChatGPT Atlas and Perplexity Comet). Forcepoint X-Labs also publicly documented 10 verified IPI payloads on live websites at https://www.forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads, spanning financial fraud, credential theft, data exfiltration, data destruction, malware distribution, social engineering, and DoS.
enrichment.patch_available: No traditional versioned patch is available because IPI is a class of attack against LLM-driven products rather than a single software defect. Google describes a continuous hardening program for Gemini and Workspace with Gemini — Gemini Model Hardening plus layered deterministic/ML/LLM-based defenses — documented at https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/ and https://blog.google/security/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks/. Google DeepMind has also published an ongoing IPI defense process (https://www.securityweek.com/google-deepmind-unveils-defense-against-indirect-prompt-injection-attacks/). Microsoft publishes Azure Prompt Shields guidance at https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/zero-trust/sfi/defend-indirect-prompt-injection.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. Google's Threat Intelligence team (Brunner, Liu, Pande) reported in the April 23, 2026 blog post a 'relative increase of 32% in the malicious category between November 2025 and February 2026' of attempts to manipulate AI systems browsing the web. Forcepoint X-Labs independently verified 10 IPI payloads on live websites (financial fraud via PayPal/Stripe lures, API-key exfiltration, and destructive commands like 'delete all files on the user's machine').
enrichment.mitigation: A layered defense approach is recommended: (1) enforce a strict data-instruction boundary — segregate and clearly mark external content as untrusted data, never as commands; (2) deterministic defenses including user confirmation prompts, URL sanitization, and tool-chaining policies; (3) ML-based and LLM-based defenses (e.g., prompt-shield style analysis and sanitization of incoming prompts); (4) Gemini Model Hardening to improve the model's internal ability to identify and ignore harmful embedded instructions; (5) Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) approval for high-risk actions; (6) principle of least privilege for agent permissions and API tokens; (7) input and output filtering (semantic filters, string-checking, the RAG Triad); (8) human and automated red-teaming; (9) participation in the Google AI Vulnerability Reward Program (VRP); (10) require deterministic validation of expected output formats.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) exploits the trust boundary between an LLM's system/user prompt and the external data the LLM retrieves while answering a query. An attacker plants malicious natural-language (or encoded) instructions inside content the LLM will later read — emails, shared documents, calendar invites, web pages, HTML metadata, document footers, etc. When the user issues a benign query, the LLM pulls the poisoned content into its context window (often via Retrieval-Augmented Generation), cannot reliably distinguish user commands from data-borne instructions, and obeys the injected commands. Attacks can be zero-click (no user interaction required beyond a normal search/summarise, as in GeminiJack) and can target agentic capabilities (tool/function calling, code execution, email send, file deletion, payments). Obfuscation techniques observed in the wild include CSS hiding (display:none, visibility:hidden, opacity:0, zero-sized text, off-screen positioning), white-on-white text, HTML comments, XML/SVG CDATA sections, data-* attribute cloaking, base64-encoded payloads decoded at runtime, and meta-tag namespace injection. Data exfiltration is typically achieved by coercing the model to issue HTTP requests (e.g., image tags) to attacker-controlled servers.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Docs, Calendar and other Workspace surfaces reachable by Gemini), Google Gemini Enterprise, Google Vertex AI Search (VAIS), Google Gemini CLI. Specific Gemini model versions referenced in research: Gemini 2.0, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Gemini 1.5 Flash. No precise build numbers were published.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public PoCs and weaponized payloads exist. (1) 'GeminiJack' zero-click IPI proof-of-concept against Gemini Enterprise / Vertex AI Search, disclosed by Noma Labs on 8 Dec 2025 (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/). (2) Forcepoint X-Labs catalogue of 10 IPI payloads observed in the wild, 22 Apr 2026 (https://forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads). (3) Cyera Research Labs PoCs for command injection and prompt injection in Google Gemini CLI, disclosed 17 Nov 2025 (https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli). (4) Unit 42 documented a live in-the-wild IPI page at hxxps://reviewerpress[.]com/advertorial-maxvision-can/?lang=en (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/). (5) Google's white paper 'Lessons from Defending Gemini Against Indirect Prompt Injections' (arXiv 2505.14534) describes end-to-end IPI exploits against Gemini.
enrichment.patch_available: No discrete vendor patch is associated with this advisory — IPI is treated as an evolving threat class addressed through continuous model and product hardening rather than a CVE-style fix. Specific underlying issues referenced have been fixed out-of-band: Google deployed updates to the Gemini Enterprise retrieval/indexing pipeline and separated Vertex AI Search from Gemini Enterprise to remediate GeminiJack (Noma Labs, Dec 2025, https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/google-fixes-gemini-enterprise-flaw/); Cyera's Gemini CLI command/prompt-injection findings were patched via Google's VRP (Nov 2025, https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli). The vendor advisory itself is the canonical reference: https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation / in-the-wild abuse of indirect prompt injection has been reported by multiple independent researchers, even though no specific APT campaign has been publicly attributed. (1) Forcepoint X-Labs documented 10 distinct IPI payloads caught in the wild (https://forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads, 22 Apr 2026). (2) Help Net Security reported a surge of IPI activity between Nov 2025 and Feb 2026 (https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/04/24/indirect-prompt-injection-in-the-wild/, 24 Apr 2026). (3) Unit 42 (Palo Alto Networks) captured a live IPI payload served on a real public web page (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/, 3 Mar 2026). (4) Noma Labs' GeminiJack was demonstrated as a zero-click attack against production Gemini Enterprise / Vertex AI Search before remediation (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/, 8 Dec 2025). (5) MITRE ATLAS tracks 'LLM Prompt Injection (Indirect)' as an observed real-world technique (https://atlas.mitre.org/). No mass exploitation by a named threat-actor group has been publicly confirmed as of 2026-07-06.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The Google blog post does not name any specific APT, ransomware, or criminal threat-actor groups exploiting indirect prompt injection (IPI) against Workspace with Gemini. Observed IPI activity has been attributed to security researchers (Noma Labs / GeminiJack, Cyera Research Labs / Gemini CLI, Forcepoint X-Labs, Unit 42 / Palo Alto Networks, academic researchers) and to opportunistic, non-attributed in-the-wild payloads (e.g., hidden instructions planted on public web pages and in documents). MITRE ATLAS classifies 'LLM Prompt Injection (Indirect)' as a technique but does not attribute it to a named actor.
enrichment.mitigation: No single patch is possible; Google prescribes a layered defence. Key controls: (1) Discovery — continuous human + automated red-teaming, Google's AI Vulnerability Rewards Program, monitoring of public AI attacks and a centralised vulnerability catalog. (2) Synthetic-data generation (Google's 'Simula') to expand attack coverage for training and evaluation. (3) Deterministic defences — user confirmation prompts for sensitive actions, URL/markdown sanitisation, suspicious-URL redaction, tool-chaining policies and a centralised Policy Engine. (4) ML/LLM defences — prompt-injection content classifiers, security-thought reinforcement, in-context defences (Spotlighting, paraphrasing, in-context learning), ShieldGemma classifier, and adversarial fine-tuning of the model itself. (5) End-user hardening — security mitigation notifications surfaced to Workspace admins and users. (6) Third-party hardening advice: treat all retrieved/RAG content as untrusted, strip/sanitise HTML and hidden text, require human-in-the-loop confirmation for any tool/function call that touches email, shell, payments or file systems, restrict outbound egress from agent runtimes, and segment agent tools by trust level.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: The primary threat class is indirect prompt injection (IPI): adversaries embed malicious natural-language instructions inside web content the AI agent ingests (malicious sites, third-party iframes, user-generated content, URL fragments, or shared documents like Google Docs/Calendar/email). When the agent's RAG or planning layer processes that content, hidden instructions hijack the agent into attacker-chosen actions (data exfiltration via disguised image requests, unauthorized purchases, deleting files, etc.). The companion concrete bug, CVE-2026-0628, is an insufficient-policy-enforcement flaw (CWE-862) in Chrome's WebView tag that lets a malicious extension use the declarativeNetRequest API to inject JavaScript into the Gemini Live side panel, achieving privilege escalation: local file/directory access, covert camera/microphone activation, arbitrary screenshots, and phishing via the trusted Gemini panel. Attack vector is network-based; user interaction required (visiting a crafted page or installing a crafted extension).
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192 (CVE-2026-0628, insufficient policy enforcement in WebView tag / Gemini Live panel, CWE-862); Google Chrome with Gemini agentic capabilities; Google Gemini Enterprise; Google Vertex AI Search; Indirect prompt injection threat class also affects Comet (Perplexity) and Copilot for Edge
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes - Multiple public PoCs available: (1) CVE-2026-0628 PoC at https://github.com/fevar54/CVE-2026-0628-POC; (2) GeminiJack zero-click PoC at https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability; (3) HashJack PoC at https://www.infosecurity-magazine.com/news/hashjack-indirect-prompt-injection/; (4) Zero-click Drive deletion PoC at https://thehackernews.com/2025/12/zero-click-agentic-browser-attack-can.html
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Chrome 143.0.7499.192 patches CVE-2026-0628 (https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0628). Google deployed mitigations for GeminiJack issues in Gemini Enterprise/Vertex AI Search prior to coordinated public disclosure on December 9, 2025 (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability). Layered agentic-browsing defenses being progressively rolled out to Chrome (https://blog.google/security/architecting-security-for-agentic).
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation by named threat actors. For CVE-2026-0628, OffSeq Threat Radar states 'No known exploits have been reported in the wild' (as of January 6, 2026) - https://radar.offseq.com/threat/cve-2026-0628-insufficient-policy-enforcement-in-g-6d67a575. Google Security's April 23, 2026 retrospective reports a 32% increase in malicious IPI detections on the public web between November 2025 and February 2026, with activity described as opportunistic rather than tied to a specific campaign - https://blog.google/security/prompt-injections-web/. All public exploitation to date comes from security-research demonstrations (Noma Labs, Unit 42, Cato Networks, Straiker, Brave), not real-world attacker campaigns.
enrichment.mitigation: Update Google Chrome to 143.0.7499.192 or later (fixes CVE-2026-0628). For agentic-browsing threats broadly: Google's layered defenses include a User Alignment Critic (second model that vets proposed actions), Agent Origin Sets (gating agent to explicit origins), mandatory user confirmation for sensitive sites and consequential actions (e.g., payments), a real-time prompt-injection classifier, 'spotlighting' (training model to prioritize user/system instructions over page content), and deterministic checks on model-generated URLs. Workspace-side mitigations add prompt-injection content classifiers, security-thought reinforcement, markdown sanitization, Google Safe Browsing suspicious-URL redaction, human-in-the-loop confirmation for risky operations, and end-user security-mitigation notifications. Until Chrome is updated: disable/audit extensions, limit Gemini/agentic features on untrusted sites, and restrict OAuth scopes granted to AI agents.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2025-48530 is an out-of-bounds read/bounds-check flaw (CWE-125) in Android's CrabbyAVIF AVIF image parser/decoder, located within the NV12 conversion path. The defect stemmed from incorrect bounds checks inside an `unsafe` Rust block, involving out-of-bounds accesses against YUV planes, the alpha plane, Y and UV planes, and chroma-width calculations. With no additional execution privileges and no user interaction required, exploitation could lead to remote code execution when chained with other bugs. Fixed via upstream commit 5262cd9befecb4f8865925c23eb543f19967e050.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Android System component (Android platform with security patch level 2025-08-01); root cause in CrabbyAVIF AVIF image parser/decoder
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept exists. The CrabbyAVIF flaw was caught pre-release as a 'near-miss' and never shipped to a public Android build. Google's Scudo hardened allocator rendered it non-exploitable by converting the overflow into a deterministic crash.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Official vendor patch is available via Android Security Bulletin August 2025 at security patch level 2025-08-01 (and 2025-08-05). Upstream CrabbyAVIF source fix: commit 5262cd9befecb4f8865925c23eb543f19967e050.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed active exploitation in the wild. NVD, SentinelOne ('Known Exploited: No'), and Feedly ('no evidence of proof of exploitation') all report no exploitation. Google's blog explicitly describes the CrabbyAVIF issue as a 'near-miss' caught before the affected build reached a public release.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply Android security patch level 2025-08-01 (or later 2025-08-05). Ensure the Scudo hardened allocator is active to deterministically convert potential memory corruption into a crash. Strategic: continue migration of new platform code to memory-safe Rust for an order-of-magnitude reduction in memory-safety vulnerability density.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection is a class of attack in which an adversary embeds hidden malicious instructions inside external content that an AI assistant ingests on the user's behalf (e.g., emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages, images). When the LLM processes that content together with the user's prompt, it cannot reliably distinguish attacker-controlled instructions from legitimate user intent, allowing the attacker to coerce the model into exfiltrating sensitive data (location, memory, contacts, calendar details), invoking tools/connectors without authorization, modifying or deleting user data, or performing other rogue actions. Unlike direct prompt injection (where the attacker types the malicious prompt themselves), the attack is delivered through benign-looking data sources the user has asked the AI to summarize or act on. Real-world examples include: (1) a calendar invite whose description contains a natural-language payload instructing Gemini to summarize meetings and call Calendar.create, leaking private schedule data (Miggo); (2) a malicious web page that uses JavaScript top-level navigation to seed fake queries into a user's Gemini search-history personalization model, then exfiltrates location/name/memory via markdown link rendering (Tenable TRA-2025-23).
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini app; Gemini in Google Workspace apps (Gmail, Docs editors, Drive, Chat); Gemini 2.5 models; Gemini Search Personalization (specifically vulnerable to Tenable TRA-2025-23); Gemini CLI (Tracebit, Cyera findings); Gemini Voice Assistant (SafeBreach Labs finding); Gemini iOS (CVE-2025-5009)
enrichment.exploit_available:
Yes - multiple public proof-of-concept exploits are available:
- Tenable TRA-2025-23: JavaScript-based PoC for Gemini Search Personalization (https://www.tenable.com/security/research/tra-2025-23)
- Miggo 'Weaponizing Calendar Invites': PoC for indirect prompt injection via Google Calendar invites (https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini)
- SafeBreach Labs: PoC exploiting Gemini Voice Assistant through instant messaging apps (https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/)
- Tracebit: Gemini CLI silent-attack PoC (https://tracebit.com/blog/code-exec-deception-gemini-ai-cli-hijack)
- Cyera Research Labs: command injection and prompt injection in Gemini CLI (https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli)
The blog post itself (June 13, 2025) notes that at publication time, 'no public PoC code exists' for the specific class of indirect prompt injection it describes.
enrichment.patch_available: Mitigations are deployed within Gemini and Gemini 2.5 (model hardening, system-prompt reinforcement, markdown sanitization, URL redaction via Safe Browsing, HITL confirmations, and end-user notifications are already in production). The proprietary prompt-injection content classifiers were described as 'in the process of rolling out' as of June 13, 2025. For the specific Tenable TRA-2025-23 finding in Gemini Search Personalization, Google rolled back the vulnerable personalization model and applied its layered prompt-injection defenses. Patch/mitigation reference: http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes - active exploitation is confirmed in the wild. Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) published the 'GTIG AI Threat Tracker' (February 2026) and earlier 'Adversarial Misuse of Generative AI' report documenting government-backed APT and IO actors actively misusing Gemini for phishing, vulnerability research, reconnaissance, payload development, IT-worker schemes, malware rewriting, and influence operations. Google's own 'AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web' (April 23, 2026) reports an increase in indirect prompt injection attempts observed on public web content. SecurityWeek (April 27, 2026) corroborates the rise. Independent researchers have additionally produced weaponized demonstrations of indirect prompt injection against production Gemini surfaces (Miggo calendar attack, Tenable TRA-2025-23 search-history exfiltration, SafeBreach Gemini Voice Assistant exploit, Tracebit Gemini CLI hijack, Cyera Gemini CLI command/prompt injection).
enrichment.threat_actors: Google's Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) has confirmed adversarial misuse of Gemini by multiple government-backed and financially-motivated actors, including: APT42 (Iran), APT41 (China), DRAGONBRIDGE (China, IO group), APT43 (North Korea), KRYMSKSKYBRIDGE (Russia), and Doppelganger (Russia). Financially-motivated cybercriminals have used jailbroken LLMs such as FraudGPT and WormGPT against Gemini. GTIG also identified experimental dropper malware tracked as PROMPTFLUX (June 2025) experimenting with LLMs for development. Additionally, public researchers (Tenable, Miggo, SafeBreach Labs, Tracebit, Cyera) have demonstrated working prompt-injection exploits against Gemini products.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's published layered defense strategy combines: (1) model hardening via adversarial training of Gemini 2.5; (2) proprietary ML-based prompt-injection content classifiers that scan files, emails and other ingested content for malicious instructions (noted as 'in the process of rolling out' at publication); (3) 'security thought reinforcement' - targeted system-prompt instructions reminding the model to ignore adversarial content; (4) markdown sanitization and suspicious URL redaction (blocking external image rendering and using Google Safe Browsing to mask malicious links); (5) a user-confirmation / human-in-the-loop (HITL) framework that requires explicit user approval before sensitive or destructive actions (e.g., deleting calendar events, sending mail, calling external tools); and (6) end-user security banners and contextual 'Learn more' notifications whenever a defense is triggered. Google Workspace administrators are advised to keep Gemini features enabled with the default controls and to follow Google's Workspace admin guidance for indirect prompt injection.

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: PRC state-sponsored APT actors gain initial access by exploiting known CVEs in network edge devices (backbone/PE/CE routers, Ivanti Connect Secure, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Cisco IOS XE) and use VPS hosts and compromised routers as stepping stones. Persistence techniques include modifying access control lists (often naming ACLs 'access-list 20'), installing SSH backdoors/authorized keys on non-standard ports, using SNMP SET requests for persistent access, establishing GRE/IPsec tunnels, and abusing the Cisco IOS XE Guest Shell container to run Python scripts (e.g., 'siet.py', 'dohost'). Credential theft is performed by enabling Cisco Embedded Packet Capture (EPC) to record TACACS+ and RADIUS authentication traffic (e.g., files like 'mycap.pcap', 'tac.pcap'), then decrypting TACACS+ packet bodies using shared secrets to recover administrator credentials; actors also modify TACACS+ server configurations to redirect authentication to attacker-controlled infrastructure. Actors enable SPAN/RSPAN/ERSPAN traffic mirroring, harvest BGP/RSVP/MIB/MPLS configuration via TFTP, brute-force or decrypt Cisco Type 5 and Type 7 passwords, and create new privileged accounts. Data is exfiltrated through GRE or IPsec tunnels so it blends with carrier-grade traffic. Evasion consists of clearing local logs, disabling log forwarding, and using Guest Shell for stealthy staging.
enrichment.affected_products: Cisco IOS XE (CVE-2023-20198, CVE-2023-20273, CVE-2018-0171 Smart Install); Ivanti Connect Secure and Ivanti Policy Secure (CVE-2024-21887, chained with CVE-2023-46805); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect (CVE-2024-3400). Targeted device classes include large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, provider edge (PE) routers, and customer edge (CE) routers.
enrichment.exploit_available: Public exploits and PoCs exist for the referenced CVEs (these are widely known, previously disclosed vulnerabilities), and the PRC threat actors are actively leveraging them against network edge devices. CISA notes that exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has NOT been observed to date — exploitation is focused on known, publicly exploited CVEs such as Cisco IOS XE (CVE-2023-20198, CVE-2023-20273), Cisco Smart Install (CVE-2018-0171), Ivanti Connect Secure / Policy Secure (CVE-2024-21887 chained with CVE-2023-46805), and Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect (CVE-2024-3400).
enrichment.patch_available: Vendor patches are available for the individually referenced CVEs from Cisco (IOS XE, Smart Install), Ivanti (Connect Secure / Policy Secure), and Palo Alto Networks (PAN-OS GlobalProtect). The AA25-239A advisory itself does not issue a single patch; rather, it consolidates TTPs and defensive guidance tied to those underlying vendor fixes. Organizations should consult each vendor's individual security advisory and CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog (https://www.cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog) for patch information.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA states these APT actors have had 'considerable success exploiting publicly known common vulnerabilities' against exposed network-edge devices, and exploitation is widely reported to still be ongoing. This is corroborated by SafeBreach, Picus Security, the BSI Salt-Typhoon CSA, and LearnGasse Security. CISA specifically states 'exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has not been observed to date' — the activity is sustained espionage via known CVEs, not n-day/0-day exploitation.
enrichment.threat_actors: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor — People's Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors (linked to MSS and PLA) running a long-term global espionage campaign targeting telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply vendor patches for the listed CVEs (prioritize Ivanti, Palo Alto, and Cisco IOS XE vulnerabilities); consult CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog. Regularly review router/network device logs for: (1) unexpected GRE tunnels pointing to foreign infrastructure, (2) unexpected external IPs in ACLs or set as TACACS+/RADIUS servers, (3) unexpected packet capture or traffic mirroring settings, and (4) unexpected virtual containers or commands within containers on the device. Implement a robust change-management process that stores, tracks, and regularly audits device configurations. Enforce SNMPv3 with strong authentication; eliminate weak/default community strings; disable unused services, Telnet, unencrypted HTTP, and non-essential ports. Verify local account authenticity and permissions; monitor for abnormal TACACS+/RADIUS server changes or redirections. Monitor Cisco Guest Shell via syslog and AAA accounting; hunt for commands like 'guestshell enable' or 'guestshell run bash'; disable Guest Shell if not required. Compare firmware/image hashes against vendor values and enable signed image enforcement. Adopt network segmentation and zero-trust principles, phishing-resistant MFA, supply-chain auditing, continuous threat hunting, and red-team exercises.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: AA25-141A describes a long-running Russian state-sponsored cyber-espionage campaign (active since at least 2022) by GRU Unit 26165 against Western logistics entities and IT/technology companies involved in coordinating, transporting, and delivering foreign assistance to Ukraine. Attack vector: spearphishing (for credentials and malware delivery), credential guessing/brute force and password spraying, SQL injection against public-facing apps, and exploitation of public-facing vulnerabilities in Microsoft Outlook (NTLM EoP via specially crafted PidLidReminderFileParameter that triggers outbound NTLM authentication), Roundcube Webmail (RCE via shell metacharacters in rcube_image.php; XSS via crafted email; SQL injection in search/search_params), and WinRAR (code execution through misleading file extensions in ZIP archives when users preview files). Post-exploitation: modification of Microsoft Exchange mailbox permissions to maintain access; exploitation of trust relationships with IT/managed-service providers; reconnaissance of IT/OT systems including ICS components for railway management; credential and AD dumping using ntdsutil, vssadmin, wmic, ADexplorer, ldap-dump.py, Certipy, Get-GPPPassword.py; lateral movement via Impacket, PsExec, and RDP; data exfiltration via OpenSSH and PowerShell; and abuse of compromised SOHO devices, IP cameras, and border-surveillance devices as proxy/operational infrastructure. Malware families deployed include HEADLACE and MASEPIE. IOCs include infrastructure domains *.accesscam[.]org and *.giize[.]com and YARA rules for Impacket tools and GENERIC_PSEXEC.
enrichment.affected_products: Microsoft Outlook for Windows (CVE-2023-23397), Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 / 1.3.17 / 1.4.12 (CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2021-44026), RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831), Microsoft Exchange, corporate VPN appliances, internet-connected IP cameras / SOHO / border surveillance devices, and ICS components for railway management. Targeted sectors include defense industry, transportation and transportation hubs (ports, airports), maritime, air traffic management, and IT services.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (for CVE-2023-23397, the primary Outlook NTLM EoP exploited in the campaign). Underlying CVEs: CVE-2020-12641 vector AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H; CVE-2023-38831 vector CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H.
enrichment.exploit_available: Public exploits and PoCs are widely available for all five referenced CVEs (CVE-2023-23397, CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2021-44026, CVE-2023-38831); the advisory notes GRU Unit 26165 exploited 'public vulnerabilities' and used publicly known tools such as Impacket, PsExec, and Certipy. Reference Impacket YARA rule: https://github.com/Neo23x0/signature-base/blob/master/yara/gen_impacket_tools.yar
enrichment.patch_available: Vendor patches exist and are publicly available for all CVEs referenced in the advisory: Outlook CVE-2023-23397 (Microsoft March 2023 Patch Tuesday update — see Microsoft Security Response Center guidance at https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2023-23397); Roundcube CVE-2020-12641 fixed in 1.4.4, CVE-2020-35730 fixed in 1.2.13/1.3.16/1.4.10, CVE-2021-44026 fixed in 1.3.17/1.4.12; WinRAR CVE-2023-38831 fixed in WinRAR 6.23. The advisory URL for the overall mitigations and patching guidance is https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA, NSA, and FBI (with international partners from the UK, Germany, Czech Republic, Poland, Australia, Canada, Estonia, and France) jointly attribute an ongoing, long-running cyber-espionage campaign to Russian GRU Unit 26165 (APT28 / Fancy Bear) targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies supporting aid to Ukraine since at least 2022. Sources confirming active exploitation: CISA Joint Cybersecurity Advisory AA25-141A (https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a; PDF at http://media.defense.gov/2025/May/21/2003719846/-1/-1/0/CSA_RUSSIAN_GRU_TARGET_LOGISTICS.PDF); FBI alert (https://fbi.gov/file-repository/cyber-alerts/russian-gru-targeting-western-logistics-entities-and-technology-companies-052125.pdf/view); Australian Cyber Security Centre advisory (https://cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/alerts-and-advisories/russian-gru-targeting-western-logistics-entities-and-technology-companies); AttackIQ response template (https://attackiq.com/2025/05/21/response-to-cisa-advisory-aa25-141a); SafeBreach coverage (https://www.safebreach.com/blog/safebreach-coverage-for-us-cert-aa25-141a-russian-gru-espionage); SOC Prime detection content (https://socprime.com/blog/detect-apt28-attacks-against-western-companies-coordinating-aid-to-ukraine); Congressional Research Service R46974.
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian GRU 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165 — also tracked in industry as APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, and BlueDelta
enrichment.mitigation: Implement Zero Trust architecture; deploy EDR; block/alert on outbound NTLM and SMB traffic to external infrastructure; enable Microsoft Attack Surface Reduction Rules (block executable content from email) and monitor for unexpected log clearing; require phishing-resistant MFA (hardware tokens) for all accounts, especially privileged and remote-access accounts; enforce account lockout/throttling and strong passwords; remove passwords from Group Policy Preferences (GPP); apply least-privilege to Microsoft Exchange mailbox permissions and audit delegate/send-as/send-on-behalf changes; isolate and monitor ICS/OT networks; for IP cameras/SOHO devices: replace out-of-support devices, apply firmware updates, disable UPnP/P2P/Anonymous Visit features, close unused ports/services (FTP, web UI), require authenticated RTSP, restrict management access to VPNs with MFA, and audit user accounts; block incoming traffic from public VPN services and outgoing traffic to hosting/API-mocking services; patch VPNs, Outlook, Roundcube, and WinRAR; use SIGMA/YARA rules (e.g., Impacket detection) and URL/domain reputation feeds; train users against spearphishing.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable. The blog describes generic browser attack-chain techniques (rendering-logic flaws, JavaScript execution, document handling, memory-safety weaknesses, sandbox escape, privilege escalation, phishing, credential theft, session abuse, AiTM, clickjacking, XSS, HTML smuggling) but does not disclose a specific vulnerability because none is named.
enrichment.exploit_available: No public PoC or weaponized exploit is referenced by the article. The post describes generic browser attack-chain techniques but does not link to any proof-of-concept code or exploit sample.
enrichment.patch_available: The article does not link to a specific vendor patch. It discusses the need to validate, test, stage, and confirm patches after vulnerability disclosure but provides no concrete patch URL.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No specific in-the-wild exploitation of an individual vulnerability is reported. The article cites aggregate statistics: Verizon 2026 DBIR (vulnerability exploitation surpassed stolen credentials as the top breach entry point in 2025) and CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report (42% of vulnerabilities exploited before public disclosure).
enrichment.mitigation: CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access - a runtime security module inside the browser using JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) to disrupt zero-day exploit chains. It blocks phishing/AiTM, protects session tokens against hijacking/MFA bypass, and prevents credential theft and data exfiltration at execution. Additional hardening: validate, test, stage, and confirm patches across managed and unmanaged devices; cover employees, contractors, and third parties; reduce reliance on patch timing alone.

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable. This is a survey/research publication from CrowdStrike, not a vulnerability bulletin. Key reported statistics: 94% of organisations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure/exfiltration; 73% cannot consistently detect cloud intrusions; 91% cannot contain cloud intrusions in real time; 95% report integration gaps with main SOC workflows; 68% take 15+ minutes to detect attacks; 47% experienced incidents/suspicious activity targeting cloud-based AI/ML in the past 12 months. No specific vulnerability mechanism, CVE, or attack vector is described.
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is associated with this publication. The referenced CrowdStrike blog post summarises findings from a survey of 1,000 global security leaders and does not reference any specific exploit code or PoC.
enrichment.patch_available: No vendor patch is associated with this publication. The CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey blog post (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/blog/crowdstrike-state-of-cdr-survey-key-takeaways/) and accompanying report (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/resources/reports/state-of-cloud-detection-and-response-survey/) are informational research publications, not patch releases for a specific vulnerability.
enrichment.active_exploitation: The report cites that 94% of surveyed organisations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure or exfiltration and 47% experienced incidents or suspicious activity targeting cloud-based AI/ML in the past 12 months. These are aggregate industry-survey statistics about cloud intrusions broadly, not confirmed active exploitation of a specific named vulnerability, CVE, or product defect.
enrichment.mitigation: The CrowdStrike blog references the vendor's complementary guidance document 'Cloud Detection and Response Survival Guide for the SOC' (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/resources/white-papers/cloud-detection-and-response-survival-guide-for-soc/) as a resource for cloud detection and response hardening. Broader themes highlighted by the survey include improving cloud visibility, reducing fragmented detection/response tooling, integrating cloud telemetry into SOC workflows, and increasing the speed of cloud attack detection and containment.

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
